abstract:
This study investigated the influence of various solvent systems on the extraction of antioxidant compounds from black mulberry (Morus nigra), blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius), and strawberry (Fragaria x ananassa). To determine the most effective extraction methods, we analyzed total phenolic content, anthocyanin levels, and overall antioxidant capacity across different extracts. Principal component analysis (PCA) was subsequently employed to provide a comprehensive overview of how solvent composition dictates extraction efficiency.

The results indicate that an acetone/water mixture (70/30, v/v) was the most effective solvent for recovering phenolic compounds across all berry types, while a methanol/water/acetic acid solution (70/29.5/0.5, v/v/v) yielded the highest anthocyanin concentrations. Optimal antioxidant capacities were achieved using different mixtures for each fruit: ethanol/water (50/50, v/v) for black mulberries, acetone/water/acetic acid (70/29.5/0.5, v/v/v) for blackberries, and acetone/water (50/50, v/v) for strawberries. These findings demonstrate that antioxidant extraction is highly sensitive to the specific solvent combinations used. Furthermore, PCA successfully differentiated extracts prepared with aqueous-organic mixtures from those obtained using pure organic solvents, highlighting the critical role of water content in maximizing phytochemical recovery.
